repo.description: The most widely used Python to C compiler
repo.name: cython

body: Introduces specialised replacements for `PyNumber_Add`, `PyNumber_Subtract` and `PyNumber_Multiply` that take care of available type information and provide fast paths for Python int and float objects found at runtime.

comment_data.body:
My intention was rather to restrict this code to the cases that we expect to be fast when going through type slots. `CYTHON_USE_TYPE_SLOTS` seemed a good indication for that. I think the `…Try…()` was mostly a relic of other things that I tried before. As you write, it can be removed if we keep the guard.

I'm not sure it's worth doing the whole dance also for the Limited API. It might be, but then it depends on the underlying implementation (which, admittedly, is CPython-only right now). Probably worth a try.
Fair enough - that's a reasonable - I imagine `PyType_GetSlot` is somewhat slower. It's probably just the `TryGetSlot` that's a little misleading then.
I changed the guard to include the Limited API in Py3.10+. Let's see.
`op` is length 1 isn't it and and could be passed as a `char` rather than a C string?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -1075,6 +1075,301 @@ static PyObject* __Pyx__PyNumber_PowerOf2(PyObject *two, PyObject *exp, PyObject
     return (inplace ? PyNumber_InPlacePower : PyNumber_Power)(two, exp, none);
 }
 
+/////////////// PyNumberBinop.proto ///////////////
+
+#if CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_PYPY || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_GRAAL || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_LIMITED_API
+
+#define __Pyx_{{op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(op1, op2)  {{op_name}}(op1, op2)
+#define __Pyx_{{inplace_op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(op1, op2)  {{inplace_op_name}}(op1, op2)
+
+#else
+
+#define __Pyx_{{op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(op1, op2)  __Pyx__{{op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(op1, op2, 0)
+#define __Pyx_{{inplace_op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(op1, op2)  __Pyx__{{op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(op1, op2, 1)
+
+static CYTHON_INLINE PyObject* __Pyx__{{op_name}}_{{type1}}_{{type2}}(PyObject *op1, PyObject *op2, int inplace); /*proto*/
+#endif
+
+/////////////// PyNumberBinop ///////////////
+//@requires: ObjectHandling.c::FormatTypeName
+
+#if !(CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_PYPY || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_GRAAL || CYTHON_COMPILING_IN_LIMITED_API)
+
+{{py: assert type1 in ('object', 'int', 'float'), type1 }}
+{{py: assert type2 in ('object', 'int', 'float'), type2 }}
+{{py: slot_name = {'+': 'add', '-': 'subtract', '*': 'multiply'}[c_op] }}
+{{py:
+def is_type(operand, expected, type1=type1, type2=type2):
+    assert operand in ('op1', 'op2'), operand
+    assert expected in ('int', 'float'), type
+    type = type1 if operand == 'op1' else type2
+    if type == expected:
+        check = f"likely({operand} != Py_None)"
+    else:
+        function = "PyFloat_CheckExact" if expected == 'float' else 'PyLong_CheckExact'
+        check = f"{function}({operand})"
+        other_type = type2 if operand == 'op1' else type1
+        if other_type == expected:
+            check = f"likely({check})"
+    return check
+}}
+
+#if CYTHON_USE_TYPE_SLOTS || __PYX_LIMITED_VERSION_HEX >= 0x030A0000
+#ifndef __Pyx_DEFINED_BinopTypeError
+#define __Pyx_DEFINED_BinopTypeError
+
+static void __Pyx_BinopTypeError(PyObject *op1, PyObject *op2, const char* op, int inplace) {
+    // op1 is either 'int' or 'float', op2 is unknown.
+    __Pyx_TypeName type_name_op1 = __Pyx_PyType_GetFullyQualifiedName(Py_TYPE(op1));
+    __Pyx_TypeName type_name_op2 = __Pyx_PyType_GetFullyQualifiedName(Py_TYPE(op2));
+    PyErr_Format(PyExc_TypeError,
+        "unsupported operand type(s) for %.2s%.1s: '%.5s' and '%.200s'",
+        op,

comment_data.body:
I can come up with a case where this contradicts Python here:

```
>>> 0.0*(2**1000000)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
    0.0*(2**1000000)
    ~~~^^~~~~~~~~~~~
OverflowError: int too large to convert to float
```

with that I don't see any reason not to just return 0.

As far as I can tell `PyNumber_Add(a, b)` only tries `a` to see if it's a sequence. I don't think `sq_concat` gets called with the reverse operands.

(This isn't based on testing though, because I don't think any of the built-in classes are would work for this)
Same here for concat - I don't think this gets reversed.
Does this need to be skipped if `!CYTHON_USE_TYPE_SLOTS and Python <3.10`? (I know no-one will actually be using that combination outside the Limited API though, but you've accounted for it earlier)

I'm slowly looking into including division, and `//` has two characters. If it stays as it is now, I agree that a `char` would be simpler and (thus) better, but it's not a big difference and not so likely that we'll stop right here.

+    type = type1 if operand == 'op1' else type2
+    if type == expected:

+    else:

+        check = f"{function}({operand})"
+        other_type = type2 if operand == 'op1' else type1
+        if other_type == expected:
+            check = f"likely({check})"
+    return check
+}}
+
+#if CYTHON_USE_TYPE_SLOTS || __PYX_LIMITED_VERSION_HEX >= 0x030A0000

+#define __Pyx_DEFINED_BinopTypeError

+static void __Pyx_BinopTypeError(PyObject *op1, PyObject *op2, const char* op, int inplace) {
+    // op1 is either 'int' or 'float', op2 is unknown.
+    __Pyx_TypeName type_name_op1 = __Pyx_PyType_GetFullyQualifiedName(Py_TYPE(op1));
+    __Pyx_TypeName type_name_op2 = __Pyx_PyType_GetFullyQualifiedName(Py_TYPE(op2));

comment_data.body:
Good call, but I'm rather inclined to consider this exception an implementation detail. It's an edge case, though. We could argue that, if `0 * x` is special enough to break the rules, then why not `1 * x` ? And if `0 * x` succeeds, then why not `1e-300 * x` ?

We could emulate it by moving the special case after the unpacking of `op2`. I doubt that the slow-down would be relevant in real code – multiplication by 0 is not going to be performance critical anywhere. It might not even be worth the branch, but it seemed so cheap to reuse the zero-object in `op1`.

I was really trying to see if large floats got rounded to infinity (which they don't).

I'm completely in favour of leaving this as an implementation detail.

> if `0 * x` is special enough to break the rules, then why not `1 * x`

The difference is that `1.0 * x` also requires a conversion to `float` (which would legitimately be an overflow error). So I don't think there's a working rule-break for that.
Right. Sequence repetition gets reversed, but concat doesn't.
It shouldn't be a heap type issue, `PyLong` is definitely a static type in Py3.9. And without that constraint, we'd always try the slot functions in the other places, so this doesn't need a guard.

>> if `0 * x` is special enough to break the rules, then why not `1 * x`
> 
> The difference is that `1.0 * x` also requires a conversion to `float` (which would legitimately be an overflow error). So I don't think there's a working rule-break for that.

Right, although I was mostly trying to avoid constructing semantics based on implementation details – under which I'd also comprise the boundary that starts raising an `OverflowError`, however reasonable it is. If we'd make it an implementation detail that `0 * x` always succeeds, then users could accidentally write code that relies on that, although from our perspective it's just an optional optimisation.

I think we can reverse the unpacking here, unsafe first, safe last. That would make it more natural to keep the special cases.

commit.message: Speed up add/sub/mul operators on int and float.
Also use reverse slot lookups in Limited API 3.10+.
Tighten the argument lengths in the error formatting string.
Fix slot function call for Limited API.
Extend matrix test to cover some object arithmetic.
Fix non-import in benchmark for Python run.
Avoid code duplication for inplace variant and use a flag variable instead.
Fix useless object back-and-forth unpacking in benchmark.
Remove slow non-pylong-internals code and distribute the special cases a bit better.

Disable the optimisation in the Limited API.
Cannot beat inlining behind a single API call.
Dispatch directly to the PyLong-slot if we know both sides are ints.
Benchmarks: Add a mixed int+float matrix benchmark.

Refactor the utility code to inline only the fast int-int and float-float cases.
Use separate functions for all other cases and separate the int and float code paths completely because most cases are handled differently for them.
Remove "sq_concat" test that Python also doesn't do.
Make sure we notice overflows when converting int to float.

title: Speed up add/sub/mul operators on int and float
